title: Integer Representation
summary: Understand how computers store unsigned integers, perform binary conversions, handle overflow, and encode characters as ASCII.
content_html:
Unsigned Integers

An unsigned integer uses all N bits to represent non-negative values. With N bits you can store 2N distinct values, ranging from 0 to 2N - 1.

N bits -> range: 0 to (2^N - 1)

1 bit:   0 to 1          (2 values)
4 bits:  0 to 15         (16 values)
8 bits:  0 to 255        (256 values)
16 bits: 0 to 65535      (65536 values)
32 bits: 0 to 4294967295 (about 4.3 billion)

Binary to Decimal Conversion

Each bit position represents a power of 2. Multiply each bit by its weight and sum:

Bit weights (8-bit):
  128   64   32   16    8    4    2    1
  2^7  2^6  2^5  2^4  2^3  2^2  2^1  2^0

Example: 01001110
  0x128 + 1x64 + 0x32 + 0x16 + 1x8 + 1x4 + 1x2 + 0x1
  = 64 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 78

Example: 11010011
  1x128 + 1x64 + 0x32 + 1x16 + 0x8 + 0x4 + 1x2 + 1x1
  = 128 + 64 + 16 + 2 + 1 = 211

Decimal to Binary Conversion

Repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders. Read the remainders from bottom (MSB) to top (LSB):

Convert 78 to binary:
  78 / 2 = 39  remainder 0
  39 / 2 = 19  remainder 1
  19 / 2 =  9  remainder 1
   9 / 2 =  4  remainder 1
   4 / 2 =  2  remainder 0
   2 / 2 =  1  remainder 0
   1 / 2 =  0  remainder 1  (MSB)
  Read bottom-up: 1001110  (or 01001110 in 8-bit)

Integer Overflow

When arithmetic exceeds the maximum value for N bits, the result wraps around. The carry bit is lost.

8-bit unsigned overflow (255 + 1):
  1111 1111  (255)
+ 0000 0001  (  1)
-----------
 10000 0000  -> carry dropped -> 0000 0000 = 0

8-bit unsigned overflow (200 + 100 = 300 -> wraps to 44):
  300 - 256 = 44
  This is why C programs can produce unexpected results!

ASCII Character Encoding

ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) maps 128 characters to 7-bit codes (stored in 8 bits). Printable characters start at code 32.

Key ASCII values (decimal):
  32  = SPACE
  48  = '0'   (digits 0-9 are 48-57)
  65  = 'A'   (uppercase A-Z are 65-90)
  97  = 'a'   (lowercase a-z are 97-122)

Examples:
  'A' = 65  = 0x41 = 0100 0001
  'a' = 97  = 0x61 = 0110 0001
  '0' = 48  = 0x30 = 0011 0000
  'Z' = 90  = 0x5A = 0101 1010

Note: uppercase + 32 = lowercase (just bit 5 differs)

Hexadecimal Shorthand

Hex provides a compact way to write binary values. One hex digit = 4 bits exactly, so 2 hex digits = 1 byte.

Hex  Binary   Decimal
 0   0000       0
 9   1001       9
 A   1010      10
 F   1111      15

0xFF = 1111 1111 = 255  (max 8-bit value)
0xAB = 1010 1011 = 10x16 + 11 = 171
0x1F = 0001 1111 = 16 + 15 = 31

exercises.title: 8-bit range
exercises.prompt: What is the maximum value that can be stored in an 8-bit unsigned integer?

exercises.title: ASCII 'A'
exercises.prompt: What is the decimal ASCII code for uppercase 'A'?

exercises.title: ASCII '0'
exercises.prompt: What is the decimal ASCII code for the character '0' (zero)?

exercises.title: N-bit capacity
exercises.prompt: How many distinct values can N bits represent?

exercises.title: Bit weight
exercises.prompt: What is the decimal value of bit position 6 (the 7th bit from the right, counting from 0)?

exercises.title: ASCII case difference
exercises.prompt: What is the difference between the ASCII codes of 'a' and 'A'?
